According to the police report, a review of Motorworks's surveillance footage revealed that five to seven offenders stole many key fobs and began stealing luxury vehicles at 6:00 in the morning. They were in the middle of stealing their second round of cars when police arrived. 27-year-old Tavarious Jackson was charged with two counts of aggravated possession of a stolen vehicle, criminal damage to property, aggravated battery to a peace officer, and aggravated fleeing and eluding. A second suspect, 23-year-old Lamont Jackson was arrested two miles south of Motorworks after crashing the second Mercedes that fled the lot. He was charged with aggravated fleeing and eluding and possession of a stolen motor vehicle. Both suspects were held on a $100,000 bond, and both suspects were on parole for prior felony convictions. According to the Illinois Department of Corrections, Tavarious received a four-year prison sentence for two counts of burglary and three counts of possessing a stolen vehicle. He was released April 2025 and is on parole through spring of 2026. Lamont received five years for burglary, aggravated reckless driving, and possession of a stolen vehicle, and is currently serving his time at Pinckneyville Correctional Center. According to the police report, the black Porsche was never located. In all, five people sustained injuries in four separate crashes caused by the group of thieves, and all involved have made a full recovery. And they didn't just steal once, they came back for seconds, same lot, same plan, and same arrogance. But that's not a random crime, that's a system. Timing is everything because early morning thefts mean low patrol presence, lower dispatch response, and open highways for quick transport. Crews like this do not operate solo. Now federal prosecutors in the Eastern District of Missouri, working with the Justice Department's violent crime and racketeering section, recently indicted 10 members of an organized theft ring called the Strikers. And this crew hit more than 20 dealerships across Eastern Missouri and Southern Illinois, stealing around 50 high-end cars. Hellcats, Trackhawks, BMWs, Audis, Mercedes, worth $3 million. These thefts aren't isolated incidents, they're symptoms of a larger well-coordinated criminal economy stretching across state lines.
